summary: Karen's Botanicals handcrafts organic skincare and bodycare products — cleansers, toners, creams, serums, oils, balms, and herbal health tonics — in small batches on the Mitchell family farm in Georgia, using 19th-century Slovak botanical recipes. It serves health-conscious women roughly 35–65 who distrust mass-market, chemical-laden beauty products and want clean, ingredient-transparent alternatives that respect aging naturally. Sales are one-off self-serve purchases (no subscription) on its own site and Amazon, with repeat buying driven by a points-based rewards program.

campaigns.name: Welcome & Onboarding
campaigns.objective: Convert a new email subscriber into a confident first purchase and get them to the 'aha' moment — the first use where clean, farm-made skincare visibly outperforms what they used before.
campaigns.trigger: Newsletter signup / list join (10%-off coupon opt-in); send within seconds.
campaigns.segment: New subscribers; branch by skin-type self-selection via the Facial Care Chart (dry/sensitive, anti-aging/mature, normal-to-oily, holistic wellness).
campaigns.rationale: Every backbone flow is currently missing and the model is fully self-serve, so a new subscriber who doesn't learn the heritage story, correct routine, and skin-type match will never reach the aha moment and won't return. The welcome send is the highest-engagement, fastest-win lever the brand has.

emails.purpose: Warm welcome + deliver the 10% code + tell the farm/heritage story + point to the one first action (match a skin type via the Facial Care Chart).
emails.subject: Welcome to the farm — your 10% off is inside 🌿
emails.preview: Two generations, one Georgia farm, and the first step for your skin.
emails.cta: Find your skin-type match & shop 10% off
keyPoints: Deliver the 10%-off welcome code prominently with a clear expiry
Tell the origin story in the brand's voice: 19th-century Slovak recipes, handcrafted in small batches on the Mitchell family farm in Georgia
Single first action — use the Facial Care Chart to match their skin type to the right cleanser/serum/moisturizer
Set expectations: a few short emails over the next two weeks on routine and results, and that a real person reads every reply
Reassure on purity — what's never in the products (synthetic fillers, parabens) to pre-empt the price-premium doubt

campaigns.name: Lead-Magnet Nurture
campaigns.objective: Move a subscriber who took the 10% coupon but hasn't purchased toward a first order by defusing the #1 objection — 'is the premium worth it vs. a drugstore product?'
campaigns.trigger: Newsletter signup with no purchase after the welcome series; sent on a ~10-day nurture cadence.
campaigns.segment: Non-purchasing subscribers below first-purchase intent; branch by the skin-type interest signaled at signup.
campaigns.rationale: The brand's stated top objection is price premium and small-batch skepticism, and acquisition (SEO, Amazon, rewards) brings in subscribers who stall on that exact doubt. A proof-and-transparency email reframes the cost before the coupon expires.

emails.purpose: Handle the price-premium objection head-on by reframing cost around ingredient transparency and what is deliberately left out, backed by substantiated proof.
emails.subject: Why our serum costs more than the one at CVS
emails.preview: It's not about what's in it — it's about what's not.
keyPoints: Reframe price: a $26 serum with named botanical ingredients vs. a $10 formula padded with synthetic fillers — cost-per-honest-ingredient, not cost-per-ounce
Lead with ingredient transparency: every ingredient readable and sourced, small-batch on the farm
Use substantiated 'clinically validated' proof and real customer results — stay within FTC efficacy-claim limits (no treat/cure language)
Spotlight one accessible best-seller matched to their skin-type interest as a low-risk first buy
Remind the 10%-off code is still active with a gentle expiry nudge

campaigns.name: Replenishment & Cross-Sell
campaigns.objective: Recover the repeat-purchase revenue lost to having no subscription by nudging a refill before consumables run out, and grow basket size by guiding the customer to the next step in their routine.
campaigns.trigger: Time since last purchase tuned to product run-out (~5–6 weeks for cleansers/lotions); usage milestone signaling routine readiness.
campaigns.segment: Past purchasers of consumable SKUs; branch high-engagement buyers into cross-sell (next routine step / premium oils) vs. straight reorder.
campaigns.rationale: This is the brand's single biggest gap: consumables cycle every 4–8 weeks but there is no auto-replenishment, so customers silently drift between cycles. A timed refill-plus-routine nudge is the most revenue-proximate flow for a no-subscription repeat-purchase model.

emails.purpose: Prompt a one-tap reorder timed to run-out, surface the rewards points already earned, and cross-sell the logical next step in the routine.
emails.subject: Running low? Your cleanser's about due 🌱
emails.preview: Reorder in a tap — and there are points waiting on your account.
keyPoints: Timed to typical run-out so the nudge feels helpful, not pushy — 'about now is when most people reach the bottom of the jar'
One-tap reorder of their last consumable purchase
Show their current rewards points balance and how close they are to the next discount tier (200–1,500 pts)
Cross-sell the next routine step matched to what they bought (cleanser → toner → moisturizer → serum) or an upsell to a premium oil
Flag low-stock items gently so repeat buyers don't hit a sold-out wall

campaigns.name: Win-Back / Re-Engagement
campaigns.objective: Reactivate lapsed buyers who missed the replenishment window and clean the list to protect deliverability.
campaigns.trigger: 60–90 days of no purchase / no engagement.
campaigns.segment: Dormant past customers; branch churned buyers (reference what they bought most) vs. never-purchased dormant subscribers (fresh value).
campaigns.rationale: No subscription and several sold-out SKUs mean first-time and repeat buyers lapse with zero automated nudge, and there's no existing re-engagement flow — a quick-to-ship win-back recovers 5–15% and shields sender reputation.

emails.purpose: Open the win-back with a warm 'we miss you', remind them of the core value, surface their unspent rewards points, and hook on a timely seasonal skin concern.
emails.subject: Your skin misses us (and we miss you)
emails.preview: Plus the rewards points sitting in your account.
emails.cta: See what's new for your skin this season
keyPoints: Warm, no-pressure 're-introduce ourselves' tone true to the heritage voice
Remind of the core value: clean, farm-made, fully transparent — the gap they felt the first time
Surface their current unspent rewards points balance as a reason to come back now
Seasonal hook tied to early-summer skin needs (lightweight hydration, sun-stressed skin) with a matched product
Low-friction return path; sets up the later pattern-interrupt and sunset emails in the series

campaigns.name: Value Newsletter
campaigns.objective: Stay top-of-mind and build authority and trust across the long repeat-purchase cycle without leaning on discounts.
campaigns.segment: All engaged contacts; suppress active onboarding (Campaign 1) and route recently-dormant contacts to Win-Back instead.
campaigns.rationale: Newsletters appear ad hoc today, yet education is exactly what this trust-and-heritage brand sells on — a consistent value-first cadence keeps a no-subscription audience engaged between purchases and assists conversions without eroding margin.

emails.purpose: Flagship edition: deliver one genuinely useful seasonal skincare ritual (80% value / 20% soft offer) under a recurring 'From the Farm' section that builds habit.
emails.subject: The forest-after-rain trick for calm summer skin
keyPoints: One useful idea per send: a seasonal early-summer skin ritual readers can do tonight
80% education / 20% soft offer — teach first, then tie softly to one matched product
Recurring 'From the Farm' segment (an herbal note or behind-the-scenes from the Georgia farm) to build a habit
Single primary CTA to read/try; invite replies to a real person
Voice-led storytelling rooted in the family legacy; no clinical jargon, no discount push
